title: Interview with Dr. Meir Stampfer
text:
i'm mayor stamford professor of
epidemiology
and nutrition at the chan school and
also professor of medicine
at harvard medical school
well i initially trained as a physician
and my goal in medical school was to
try to do preventive medicine so i was
looking for a specialty that would
enable me to do that
um but they're hard to find
luckily in my very last year of medical
school
i stumbled upon epidemiology and
knew that that was the track for me
so my research career has been
pretty long and varied i started off in
cardiovascular epidemiology
but then being closely connected to the
nurse's health study
and helping to found the health
professionals follow-up study and nurses
health study 2
and the physicians health study those
all
were cancer-related cohorts so my
efforts turned more and more towards
studying cancer i've been involved in
cancer research in a variety of
cancer sites including breast cancer
colorectal cancer
and most recently my main work in cancer
has been
in prostate cancer and in brain cancer
i was really drawn to the school of
public health
to learn epidemiologic methods in detail
even back then the department was very
strong in methods
and over the years it's become stronger
and stronger
my goal has always been to expand the
range
of practical methods and
approaches that we can take for
prevention
very little prevention work had been
done
in cancer epidemiology so that was the
big draw for me
as a means to expand that field
there are many ways in which the cancer
epidemiology program that school of
public health stands out
one is the amazing
resources that are available for cancer
research
these include the cohorts that i
described earlier
there's no comparable
population resource anywhere in the
world with repeated dietary assessments
repeated assessments of
physical activity and medication use and
a whole host of other exposures
with very high rates of follow-up
so that is quite unique we're part of
the
dana-farber harvard cancer center so
we have close by
experts in the diagnosis
and treatment of cancer of every sort
and
the world's authorities are just right
around the corner from us
and they're ready and willing and happy
to help
nothing comes close to the opportunities
really
uh sounds like bragging and i suppose it
is but
it's true
i think when i first came to harvard i i
felt a little intimidated
that how would people receive me and uh
how would how would i fit in as a new
student
and i found the atmosphere back then
and it's really remained and even grown
of
welcoming and helping
nurturing despite
all these brilliant minds or maybe
because of
brilliant minds it really is a sense
of community and
a commonality of values to
improve public health so that that
really makes it a
a special place
